data.title: QQQI: Strong Outperformance Potential (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Earnings season is about to kick off next week, and the tech sector especially seems primed for a slew of record results: recent deal activity in the data center sector strongly tilts the odds in favor of impressive Q3 tech earnings which suggests that tech-oriented covered call ETFs such as the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI) could also be doing really well.

I like that the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF has a strong NAV return profile and is concentrated in a few major tech names such as Nvidia (NVDA). The GPU maker has made a number of deals with major companies lately -- including Intel (INTC), CoreWeave (CRWV) and OpenAI -- and estimates for the firm's Q3 EPS have been rising steadily. Since Nvidia and other top AI names are overweighted in the QQQI and the ETF offers a yield in excess of 13%, I believe the investment proposition for the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF is stronger than when I last covered the ETF.

Previous rating

The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF was a buy for me in June -- A Tech-Focused Covered Call ETF With AI Upside -- because it delivered strong net asset value returns, despite a relatively short performance history, and distributed strong monthly dividends to investors. Besides a main focus on income, I believe the QQQI is an especially appealing investment now that the tech sector will soon report earnings for the third-quarter. Shares of tech companies have surged lately, and higher (implied) volatility tends to lead to higher option income for covered call ETFs, so I believe the QQQI is appealing both from a NAV growth as well as a yield perspective.

The number of deals in the data center industry has soared lately, and EPS estimates, especially for semiconductor companies, are rising broadly... meaning the QQQI could potentially benefit via sustained NAV growth. Expecting a strong earnings season for Q3 in the tech sector, I am upgrading shares of QQQI to strong buy.

Strong Nvidia and mag-7 concentration, outperformance vs. larger ETF rivals

What I like most about the QQQI ETF is that the ETF's portfolio is heavily tilted towards technology companies. The top ten holdings include all Magnificent 7 stocks, and the ETF has a strong overweighting of the world’s largest AI GPU company, Nvidia, which has an investment share of almost 10%.

Nvidia recently reached new all-time highs and a market cap exceeding $4.5 trillion for the first time… which is why Nvidia has shot up to the number one rank in the ETF's portfolio.

The breakdown of QQQI's investment portfolio looks like this...

Compared to other top-ranked covered call ETF choices -- such as the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) and the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) -- the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF is a relatively small fund, managing just about $5.4B in net assets.

The JEPQ is significantly larger than QQQI and QYLD, having a total investment volume of $30.9B. In terms of mag-7 exposure, the QQQI ranks between the JEPQ and QYLD, with an investment percentage of 44.2%. Like QYLD, the QQQI also highly values its Nvidia exposure… in both cases the AI chip giant represents approximately 10% of the ETF’s total investment volume. Both the QQQI and the QYLD have the most concentrated portfolios with top ten investments representing 53% and 54% of all investments.

The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF has a very solid return profile even though it was only kicked off a little more than one-and-a-half years ago, in January 2024. Since then, the QQQI has achieved a net asset value return of 21.08%. In the last 1-year period, QQQI has generated a NAV return of 21.8% amid a strong performance, especially in the tech sector, where companies like Nvidia and Alphabet (GOOG) achieved new record highs. For comparison, JEPQ achieved a net asset value return of 17.1%, while QYLD recorded a NAV gain of only 7.18% for investors.

There are reasons to be optimistic that the Q3 earnings season could be a catalyst for the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF to reach new highs. One reason is that EPS estimates for Nvidia, QQQI's largest holding, have risen drastically... which is a result of persistent demand for AI-optimized GPUs. Nvidia's EPS estimates for next year have surged way above $6.00 per-share, implying ~42% Y/Y earnings growth. What this implies, in my opinion, is that the GPU sector especially has strong outperformance potential that QQQI, due to its mag-7 overweighting, is set to benefit from.

Covered call strategy to complement long term NAV growth

Obviously, investors that want tech exposure, and specific AI exposure in a red-hot data center market, could simply own a technology-oriented ETF such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) or the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG). Both of these ETFs offer investors uncapped upside potential, as opposed to QQQI.

However, a covered call ETF like QQQI exists for the specific purpose of monetizing a long position in technology stocks, and the QQQI currently yields an impressive 13.5%… giving the ETF a considerable yield advantage of more than 3 PP compared to its lower-yielding covered call ETF rivals. Investors that want to benefit from both long exposure to tech and generate a double-digit yield may want to consider the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF.

QQQI’s valuation

The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF has a relatively short performance history, as it was incepted only in January 2024, but has so far done very well for investors.

On a total return basis, the QQQI has outperformed JEPQ by 6.22 PP since its inception and the QYLD by 21.32 PP. QQQI is therefore, despite its young life, ranked at the top of the performance chart in its segment... which obviously makes the QQQI a compelling investment candidate for investors that expect a strong earnings season for the tech sector.

Risks with QQQI

The biggest risk for the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF is that it has not yet proven itself over a longer stretch of time, especially during a period of tech sector underperformance. Further, as a covered call ETF, QQQI has 'capped upside' which is the consequence of writing call options for premium income. Additionally, the QQQI, by virtue of investing in U.S. large-caps, and Magnificent 7 stocks especially, has sector concentration risk, which could result in serious underperformance should the tech sector rotate out of favor with investors.

Final thoughts

The NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF is a compelling choice for investors that want to have long exposure to some of the most compelling U.S. large-cap technology companies, benefiting from AI, as well as monetize this exposure through a covered call strategy. QQQI has an aggressive positioning in GPU maker Nvidia and other Magnificent 7 stocks, which could make the ETF a good investment option for investors that have strong expectations for the upcoming earnings season in the tech sector... especially with regard to Nvidia... and that want to lock in a leading distribution yield north of 13%. Further, the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF has actually continued to outperform its much larger covered call ETF rivals JEPQ and the QYLD... and may continue to be an outperformance candidate.

data.title: Park Aerospace: Great Balance Sheet, Expensive Stock
data.description:
Thesis

Back in October of last year, I rated Park Aerospace (NYSE: NYSE:PKE) as a Hold, and I repeated that same rating in January. My main reason was that the company had a strong balance sheet, with no long-term debt and a lot of cash, but its profits and factory use weren’t as good as they should be. Profit margins were shrinking, and even though management said there was new demand from GE Aerospace (GE), it still seemed too early to jump in.

Subsequently, I missed coverage on the company and the stock ended up dropping over the next couple of quarters. But since the start of this summer (above, see the mid-RSI-range in June, followed by the parallel channel breakout in July), things finally started to show some real improvement. Now, Park just released its Q2 earnings yesterday, and after going through the numbers and the call, my research argues that the company’s fundamentally strong, but the stock’s overpriced.

Solid Q2 Delivery and Production Balance

In this latest second quarter Park Aerospace reported $16.381 million in sales and a gross profit of $5.116 million, giving it a 31.2% gross margin. The company’s adjusted EBITDA came in at $3.401 million, or a 20.8% margin; for context, management had expected $15 to $16 million in sales and $3.0 to $3.4 million in EBITDA based on guidance from the previous quarter, so the results came in slightly above the projected ranges.

Source: Seeking Alpha

The newly expanded Kansas plant continued to weigh on profit margins, just as management expected, but the company still succeeded to stay above the 30% gross margin mark, which leadership sees as the minimum for healthy performance. Production and sales were in balance, so the company didn’t have to dip into unfinished inventory, meaning there was no hit to the bottom line this quarter.

Moving on to the product mix next, during the quarter, Park sold $1.65 million worth of C2B fabric at a low profit margin (I covered this fabric a bit more last October), while also shipping $415,000 of ablative materials made from that same C2B fabric, and those came with much higher margins. Defense customers are still stockpiling the C2B fabric, which temporarily pushes down margins until that material is used to make finished products. On the bright side, the C2B material has now been requalified at a major customer, covering about 90% of the required specifications.

Testing on the remaining batch is still in progress and could take another nine to twelve months to finish. Management pointed out that production for the already-approved portion is back to normal levels and should see some extra growth as more material gets converted. Missed shipments jumped to $510,000, but that was because of customer certification and testing delays, not shipping problems or weak demand, so it’s more of a timing issue than a sales problem.

It was a nice surprise to see that tariffs didn’t really make a difference overall. The CFO said there were only about $1,700 in total tariff and tariff-related costs during the period, and those were mostly offset by price adjustments passed on to customers. On the bigger picture, Airbus sped up its aircraft deliveries in September because more engines became available. That’s good news for Park, since it’s the only supplier for parts used in the LEAP-1A engines that power the A320neo planes. According to Reuters, Airbus delivered 73 aircraft in September, and there were fewer planes waiting around without engines (gliders), matching management’s view that the supply issues are finally easing.

Defense and GE Programs

Defense is still the company’s other major growth driver. There’s record-high demand for missile systems, and, keep in mind that the company's the only distributor in North America for that ArianeGroup’s C2B fabric, which is used in heat-resistant (ablative) materials.

Back in March, Park agreed to advance €4.587 million (about $5 million) to Ariane as a prepayment for future orders to help them increase production of the C2B fabric. So far, €1.376 million of that amount has already been paid. Management agreed to pick up half the tab for a €700,000 capacity study, about $410,000, which will be jotted down next quarter as a one-time expense. Then, in September, the U.S. Army handed Lockheed Martin (LMT) a $9.8 billion multi-year deal to build nearly 2,000 PAC-3 missile interceptors. It’s another reminder that demand is climbing for the kind of C2B-based materials Park makes.

GE Aerospace program revenue came in at $7.5 million in Q2, slightly above the earlier estimate of $6.7 to $7.2 million. Management now expects GE program sales of $7.5 to $8.0 million in Q3 and between $27.5 and $29.0 million for fiscal 2026. That’s a bit lower than the previous forecast of $28 to $32 million, which had been based on the customer’s production schedule. The new outlook instead reflects Park’s own order backlog and booking pace, with Q3 already fully booked and Q4 partly booked.

Outside of GE, materials used for lightning strike protection on the Passport 20 engine are expected to add around $500,000 per year as usage grows, and more engine certifications are currently in progress.

The company finished the quarter with $61.6 million in cash and marketable securities after making a $4.9 million transition tax payment. A great feature is that they have no long-term debt. Even so, the way things look, management made the call to continue not having share buybacks.

And lastly, they reaffirmed their Q3 fiscal 2026 outlook, calling for $16.5 to $17.5 million in sales and $3.7 to $4.1 million in adjusted EBITDA. They also said total fiscal 2026 revenue should be over $70 million.

Premium Valuation, Small Buffer

Right now, the stock looks expensive to me with a non-GAAP P/E for the past 12 months at 43.3, which is much higher than Park Aerospace’s five-year average of about 29 and the industry median of around 21.

The GAAP P/E for the same period is 61.2, compared to a five-year average of about 38. That means the stock is trading roughly 49% to 61% higher than its own historical averages.

Overall, the company’s making more money, but not fast enough to justify how expensive the stock’s gotten. Revenue and EBITDA grew 16–18% from last year, but EPS only rose 4%, and long-term growth rates (CAGRs) are still weak or even negative. My take is that unless future EPS starts increasing more quickly, helped by more defense-related business and better output from GE programs, the stock’s P/E ratio will probably drop back closer to its historical average.

The EV/Sales cross-check points the same way. At 5.55x TTM sales, Park’s stock is trading much higher than its five-year average of about 3.47 and the industry median of around 2.12. The risk is basically that the high valuation could stay in place if the company keeps moving toward more profitable products like ablatives and if production of LEAP engine parts continues to ramp up smoothly. On the other hand, the stock could slip if development is slow, customer testing continues to postpone delivery, or if big projects like as the 777X encounter further obstacles.

To emphasize the 'testing' risk mentioned during the conference call, CEO Brian Shore pointed out:

It's nothing we can do about it, not our fault or anything like that. But sometimes it just delays insurance of certification and engineering work and testing delays. So that had a meaningful impact upon our shipments in Q2.

And when talking about the 777X program, Shore said:

Boeing had previously announced that the airplane program was on track for certification in late '25 and entry into service in '26, when the Boeing CEO recently stated the certification program is falling behind schedule. The CEO further stated the aircraft and the engine and GX engines, GE9X right -- GE9X engine are really performing quite well and that the potential delay in certification was being caused by increasingly deliberate FAA scrutiny.

In the meantime, PKE’s dividend looks healthy (see growth rate stats along with dividend cash flow data since 09' above), but it’s not exactly a guarantee of safety. The dividend yield’s 2.4%, below the five-year average of about 3.2%. That tells you investors are already paying up for the company’s solid balance sheet and reliable payout record. The company has around that $60 million in cash and no debt, and free cash flow has moved up compared to last year. However, capital spending is climbing quickly, and working capital could fluctuate as the defense business builds up inventory. At this higher valuation compared to both its own history and industry peers, the company will need to show steady EPS growth and a smooth shift from C2B stockpiles to higher-margin materials over the next few quarters.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’d keep PKE rated as a Hold. The company’s still in good shape overall, it has no debt, steady profit margins, and strong demand from defense projects. I've since purchased the stock in the spring, but speaking to new investors now, the stock price is already high compared to both its own past levels and similar companies. Earnings growth still hasn’t caught up with revenue growth, and capital spending keeps going up. At today’s prices, I don't think there’s not much room for the stock to climb higher.

data.title: Group 1 Automotive: Finally Time To Temper Expectations (Downgrade)
data.description:
Perhaps my favorite company in the automotive retail space has been Group 1 Automotive (NYSE:GPI). In addition to shares of the company trading on the cheap, management has a history of growing the business largely through acquisitions. Recently, profitability and cash flows have been a bit mixed. And a lot of that can be attributable to more challenging market conditions. But with the overall valuation side of things still intact, and management continuing to push additional growth as time goes on, I believe that it still represents a good opportunity for investors.

For context, the last article that I published about the firm was published back in May of this year. And since then, things have not gone exactly well, with the stock dipping by 0.5% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 15.4%. But since I originally rated it a "Strong Buy" in December 2021, shares have generated a return for investors of 134%. That's roughly triple the 46.9% rise that the S&P 500 has seen over the same window of time. Even after all of this upside, I believe that this is still a compelling opportunity. But because of how the valuation of the business has changed, I do think downgrading it to a solid "Buy" makes sense right now.

Still great, but not as great

The newest data that investors have regarding Group 1 Automotive covers through the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. This massive player in the automotive retail space, with operations spread across 17 states in the United States and 67 towns and cities throughout the UK, continues to grow at a rather impressive pace. Revenue, for instance, came in at $5.70 billion. That represents a nice increase over the $4.70 billion the company reported the previous year. This rise, according to management, was driven by growth across all major revenue categories.

For starters, new vehicle retail sales jumped 15.7% year over year from $2.36 billion to $2.74 billion. This rise, according to management, was attributable to a 17% jump in the number of retail new vehicles sold, from 47,661 to 55,763. In addition to this, there was a slight rise in the average price per unit sold of 1.1%, with that pricing climbing from $49,996.00 to $50,557. There were other areas of growth as well. Used vehicle retail sales jumped 27.2% from $1.45 billion to $1.85 billion. This was attributable to a 22.3% jump in the number of units sold, from 49,260 to 60,240, and it was also thanks to a 4.1% increase in pricing, from $29,501 to $30,713.

As the image above illustrates, while a lot of the company's revenue comes from used retail sales, most of its profitability comes from its other activities. Used vehicle wholesale revenue is notoriously unprofitable. But it is worth noting that its revenue did jump 57% year over year from $104.3 million to $163.8 million thanks to a rise in the number of units sold from 11,819 to 17,030. But the real driver of any sort of potential profit improvement would involve its parts and service activities and its finance and insurance operations. Year over year, parts and service sales jumped 27.1%, from $316.8 million to $402.8 million, while revenue for the finance and insurance products increased 18.8%, from $200.1 million to $237.8 million.

As great as these increases in revenue were to see, most of the expansion that the company saw came from acquisitions. For instance, same-store new vehicle retail sales increased a more modest 5.4%, while used vehicle retail sales on a same-store basis expanded by 6.7%. Similarly, smaller increases on a same-store basis came from its parts and service activities and its finance and insurance products. Speaking of acquisitions, in 2024 alone, the company benefited from $3.9 billion worth of revenue acquired. This is the annualized revenue it expects to get from the firms that it purchased. And management has not let up this year.

The latest move announced by management came in August of this year when the company acquired what it called a high-volume luxury brand dealership based out of Atlanta, Georgia, that focuses on the Mercedes-Benz brand. Total revenue from this dealership should be about $210 million on an annualized basis. And with that purchase, the company should now have 259 automotive dealerships, 324 franchises, and 39 collision centers spread throughout the United States and the UK. That purchase on its own brings the total year-to-date acquisitions up to $640 million of annualized revenue.

The growth in revenue that the company exhibited was great. But the same cannot be said of bottom-line results. Net income barely budged, rising from $138.2 million to $140.5 million. Based on the data provided, it appears as though the company saw small contractions in gross profit per vehicle sold. For new vehicles, the decline was 0.3%, from $3,568 to $3,557, while for used vehicles it was 2.3%, from $1,638 to $1,600. The bigger problem for the company, however, ended up being a jump in its selling, general, and administrative costs from $497.2 million to $646.1 million. This took the metric up from 64.9% of sales to 69%. Management blamed this on the acquisition of new stores and an increase in expenses when it came to existing locations. It seems this was largely the result of higher employee related costs and certain unnamed third-party services that the company paid for.

The good news is that other profitability metrics for the business held up better. Operating cash flow went from negative $124.1 million to positive $251.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $157.4 million to $200.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $269.3 million to $289.8 million. In the chart above, you can see results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024 while in the image below you can see the different items that contributed to this increase. All told, this illustrates that the second quarter on its own was not a one time thing. The company continues to expand nicely on the top line, though bottom line results have become increasingly mixed in light of rising costs.

Long term, I do believe that Group 1 Automotive will be a successful endeavor that will create a lot of value for investors. The fact of the matter is that this is an incredibly fragmented market with great growth potential. Management pegs the total used vehicle space in the United States at about 37 million unit sales each year. That's based on data from 2024, though we have historically seen a range of between 36 million units and 41 million units. Meanwhile, the new vehicle space in the United States is even larger, even though the number of sales there comes in at around 16 million units annually. In total, this is a market that's worth about $9.97 billion, which is comfortably above the $6.64 billion opportunity that exists in the used retail space.

Naturally, as the firm continues to expand in these areas, it has the opportunity to capture the higher margin opportunities in the parts and service, as well as in the finance and insurance categories. According to management, last year, the parts and service space was worth about $2.49 billion. Unfortunately, management did not provide any detail regarding how large the finance and insurance category is. What we do know, however, is that in 2024, the gross profit in this space amounted to about $2,368 per retail unit. That implies a big upside for Group 1 Automotive as well as for other players in the space.

If we project out financial performance for the company based on what we have seen already this year, we would get net profits of about $467.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $812.1 million, and EBITDA of $1.14 billion. Using these estimates, and using the results the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how the company is valued. On an absolute basis, I would actually say that this is quite attractive, especially on a forward basis for 2025. But relative to other similar firms, shares are not as cheap as they once were.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Group 1 Automotive 12.1 7.0 9.4
Sonic Automotive (SAH) 16.4 5.0 9.4
Asbury Automotive Group (ABG) 9.0 6.3 8.8
Lithia Motors (LAD) 9.3 10.7 11.0
AutoNation (AN) 13.8 29.5 12.6
Penske Automotive Group (PAG) 11.9 11.7 15.2

In the table above, I stacked Group 1 Automotive up against five other similar enterprises. While only one of the companies was cheaper than it is, and another was tied with it on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, three of them were cheaper on a price-to-earnings basis, while two were cheaper when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow approach. When I last wrote about the company, it was the second most expensive relative to earnings. But it was the second cheapest relative to operating cash flow and the cheapest when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach. So on a relative basis, shares have gotten a bit more expensive.

This alone would normally not cause me to downgrade the company. But it was a meaningful factor. Another issue at play that tipped things in the direction of a downgrade was the economic outlook for the automotive space. According to Cox Automotive, the world's largest automotive data and insights provider, tariffs are poised to have a meaningful impact on vehicles. The average cost impact for all imported vehicles is estimated to be about $5,500 per unit. There will also be an increase of approximately $1,000 per vehicle for those that are assembled here in the United States. But it is important to keep in mind that this does not reflect changes in steel and aluminum prices, which could also rise because of the tariff situation. Altogether, we are looking at a 4% to 8% increase in the price on all vehicles, which will bleed into the used market as well because of the increased demand that it will create for those. In fact, Cox Automotive specifically pointed out that this price increase is for both new and used vehicles alike.

Near term, this does not mean that we will see a worsening in terms of the demand for vehicles. In fact, Cox Automotive recently increased its guidance for new vehicle retail sales from between 15.6 million and 15.7 million up to 16.1 million. This is likely attributable to tariff policies moderating to some extent and companies adjusting to this new environment. But beyond this year, it is my contention that conditions will worsen. As I've detailed in other articles like this one and this one, I believe that the U.S. economy is heading toward a recession. And if that comes to pass, especially in an environment where prices are on the rise, firms such as Group 1 Automotive will almost certainly be negatively affected.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I still believe that Group 1 Automotive offers investors attractive upside. But I don't necessarily think it is a compelling opportunity compared to what it was previously. Market conditions are worsening, and profitability is growing at a slower pace than revenue because of higher costs. When you add all of this together, I believe that downgrading it to a solid "Buy" is the appropriate choice.

data.title: China Overseas Mining 2025 - Gold And Energy Transition Metals
data.description:
In December 1988, the Channar iron ore mine in Western Australia, then owned jointly by Rio Tinto Group (RIO) and Sinosteel Corp., embarked on its first stage of development. The project involved international financing and marked China's inaugural overseas mining investment. A decade later, in 1998, China completed its first overseas nonferrous mine transaction when China Nonferrous Mining Corp. Ltd. acquired the Chambishi copper mine in Zambia. Today, propelled by the "Go Out" policy, an increasing number of Chinese companies are actively exploring international opportunities. Those that have achieved success are further expanding their global presence. This investment strategy has significantly enhanced China's attributable production of gold and key energy transition metals.

Africa, Latin America focus of China's overseas M&A

A 2021 study identified a growing emphasis on China's mining M&A in Africa and Latin America, particularly during the 2010s. Data from 2020 onward further corroborates this trend, with Chinese companies' M&A activities in Africa and Latin America comprising 49% and 30%, respectively, of their $14.6 billion of total overseas M&A spending till 2025 year to date. This indicates a deepening presence in these regions. Moreover, the M&A data highlights a strong interest in gold and key energy transition metals, with gold, copper and lithium accounting for 86% of the transactions.

Rising gold investments

Since 2020, China's overseas M&A spending has consistently surpassed $2 billion a year. However, after China Ex-work battery lithium carbonate prices dropped almost 90% from the peak in November 2022 at $79,650/t, the focus has shifted. Overseas lithium trades slowed after 2021, while gold investments surged in 2024, culminating in six deals worth $1.7 billion.

Among major Chinese mining companies, Zijin Mining Group (OTCPK:ZIJMF)(OTCPK:ZIJMY) leads in overseas investment, contributing approximately 28% of the total M&A spending since 2020. As a pioneer in global expansion, Zijin Mining has adhered to its principle of "leveraging world-class projects and prioritizing resources. "Although rising gold prices have also pushed asset values up and raised the threshold for M&A, Zijin Mining continues its overseas ventures, completing significant transactions in Peru, Ghana and Kazakhstan. Shandong Gold Mining Co. Ltd. (OTC:SDGMF) and Zhaojin Mining Industry Co. Ltd. are also actively acquiring stakes in gold assets. As the emphasis on critical minerals increases, noncritical minerals, such as gold, continue to present promising opportunities. Our latest Gold Commodity Briefing Service expects a continued bull run of gold prices in 2026, with a projection of $3,897 per ounce.

Copper production success

China's overseas copper investments have yielded substantial results. The country's attributable share of global mined copper production increased from 10% in 2016 to 19% in 2024, while its overseas copper production surged 369% during the same period, reaching approximately 2.6 million metric tons and surpassing domestic output. According to our Copper Commodity Briefing Service, while China's domestic mined copper production accounts for only 8% of the world's total, its smelter-grade copper output represents 48%. This disparity highlights China's reliance on overseas copper resources to satisfy its robust demand. China Nonferrous Mining was among the first to explore overseas copper resources, paving the way for other companies, such as CMOC Group (OTCPK:CMCLF) and Zijin Mining, to join the race for copper mining.

Increased attributable production has enabled Chinese companies to achieve higher revenue. CMOC's Tenke Fungurume and Kisanfu mines have significantly boosted production; the two operations combined currently yield 600,000 metric tons per year of copper. The company plans to advance its expansion, with preliminary exploration underway at Tenke Fungurume West and Kisanfu Phase II. Meanwhile, the Kamoa-Kakula mine's third concentrator commissioning in May 2024 has driven Zijin Mining's copper growth. Kamoa Copper SA, a joint venture between Ivanhoe Mines Ltd. and Zijin Mining, expects copper production at the Kamoa-Kakula mine to reach 400,000 metric tons in 2025, despite the mine experiencing underground seismic activity.

The success stories of CMOC and Zijin Mining are likely to inspire more Chinese companies to pursue world-class copper assets. Using the company-level cost curve, companies whose equity production in 2024 was at least 100,000 metric tons higher than in 2020 have been identified. We have also outlined these companies' 2020 production and all-in-sustaining margin with dotted boxes in the figure below, with these two major companies demonstrating significant production growth and increased unit profits.

China's lithium refining advantage

The conversion of lithium concentrate into lithium hydroxide or lithium carbonate is crucial for battery production. As of Sept. 23, 2025, the difference between the Platts lithium spodumene 5.5% concentrate CIF China price and the lithium hydroxide delivered duty paid (DDP) China price — on a lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE) basis — stood at $3,610 per metric ton. Platts is part of S&P Global Commodity Insights. This indicates that lithium hydroxide producers must manage their concentrate conversion costs below this threshold to maintain profitability. Outside China, few processing plants have managed to keep refining costs under $4,000/t. For instance, IGO Ltd.'s Kwinana plant reduced its unit refining costs significantly within a year by the June quarter of 2025, but it still exceeds $10,000/t. In contrast, due to fierce domestic competition, maintaining unit conversion costs below $3,000/t is normal in China. This cost efficiency has become a lifeline, ensuring China's acquisition of overseas hard rock lithium mines remains economically viable. In 2024, China's attributable lithium production ranked highest globally, accounting for 33.4% of the global total. Notably, its overseas output reached 238,844 metric tons LCE, with six hard rock lithium mines in Africa acquired and commissioned after 2021.

Continued global mining journey

China's overseas mining ventures are expected to continue, evidenced by its nickel investments in Indonesia following the 2020 nickel ore export ban. Chinese companies have expanded smelting capacity in Indonesia, demonstrating that even with policy restrictions, China's substantial demand will continue to drive its pursuit of overseas mineral resources.

China has gained an advantage in the supply side of energy transition minerals such as copper and lithium, benefiting from years of consistent investment. As more Chinese companies engage in global ventures, they are also investing in bauxite, graphite, manganese and other minerals. This diversification indicates that China's global mineral resource development will continue to grow, potentially leading to even greater stories of expansion in the future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Waste Your Time Timing Bubbles: Subscriptions Make This Market Nothing Like 1999
data.description:
Subscriptions are the new taxes for the S&P 500

Boy oh boy, my podcast and media content feeds have turned the market's (NYSEARCA:SPY)(NYSEARCA:DIA)(NYSEARCA:IVV)(NYSEARCA:VOO)(NASDAQ:QQQ) bearish bubble dial up to 99. It's fair to make a lot of comparisons to 1999; we're trading along the same CAPE ratio trends, price to sales, and price to cash flow, all of which have similarities. Earnings growth is quite a bit different, however, as is earnings resiliency.

In the year 2000, Exxon Mobil (XOM) was the top dog, while Cisco (CSCO), Microsoft (MSFT), and Intel (INTC) were the only tech companies in the top 10 at the time, and only one SaaS company. Not much in the way of subscription-based revenue. The rest were quite cyclical in nature. Now let's take a look at 2025:

70%, or 7 out of 10, of the top 10 companies are tech-related and all either have or have the goal of implementing subscription-based models. Even if we were to dig into Walmart (WMT) or Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A), I'm sure you will find subscription based models [Walmart+, utilities, and insurance for Berkshire]. With the advent and growth of credit cards and autopay, subscription based models are not only sticky, they are getting to be as reliable as rent or taxes.

Ask yourself this question: in 1999, how many things did you have set up with autopay? How many things did you mail in a check for? I am betting the latter [mailing in a check] was the more common payment method of the two at that time. Breaking the relationship was much easier. Now, checks are not worth the paper they're printed on and are nearly obsolete. Lines of credit make for instant access to your balance sheet by these companies unless you either lose your creditworthiness or take the time out of your tired, post 10 hour work-shift evening to cancel your recurring subscription because you found something else. There has never been a better time to be a company running a subscription based model; everyone wants in.

Unsurprisingly, credit card payments are expected to rise pretty consistently over the next decade. As more consumers have credit, companies will have more and more instant access to your consumer balance sheet.

The market follows earnings

The market follows earnings. Most of us get our market exposure via ETFs or mutual funds that are modeled after the S&P 500 (SP500). We can see when charting the level of the S&P 500 index with the earnings per share growth of the index, the two follow each other both in terms of upswings and downswings. If earnings recede for long periods, the stock market crumbles. If earnings keep growing, the market tends to stay resilient.
